entities.example_mention: The Champlain Canal was NR listed as a district in 1976.
Head of tidal navigation on the Hudson River.
Lock C1 is on the west (Waterford) bank of Hudson at the head of a short land-cut.
Dam C-1 (DEC 225-4372) traces a zig-zag course across the river.
Lock C2 bisects a rocky island in the Hudson River between the Mechanicville Hydroelectric Plant and a fixed crest overflow dam.
Mechanicville Hydroelectric Plant (FERC P-6032)
The powerplant and dam (DEC #225-0102) were constructed by the Hudson River Power Transmission Company in 1897.
Lock C3 was constructed by Shanley-Morrissey under Contract 68.
The dam (DEC #225-0119) predates Barge Canal construction and was built to supply power and process water to paper mills.
Lock C4 was built by Shanley-Morrissey under Contract 68.
Stillwater Fixed Crest Dam (Dam C-4)
Lock C5 is at the lower end of a mile-long land cut that leads to the pool behind Northumberland Dam.
Thompson Dam - Fixed Crest Dam C-5
Lock C6 was built by Sunderstrom & Stratton under Contract 3.
Crocker Reef Dam (2 Contributing Structures)
Crocker Reef Guard Gate (1 Contributing Structure)
Lock C7 has a 10' lift to the north with downstream approach wall on the east side.
Lock C8 raises boats to the summit level between the Hudson and Lake Champlain/St. Lawrence drainage basins.

aliases: Old Champlain Canal / Glens Falls Feeder
entities.example_mention: Delivers water to summit level of Champlain Barge Canal from Feeder Dam on the Hudson.
Lock C9 is at the northern end of the summit level.
Lock C11 has a 12' lift to the south.
Lock C12 marks the northern end of the Champlain Canal and the entry into Lake Champlain.
Whitehall Terminal (1 Contributing Structure, 1 Contributing Building)
The terminal building transferred to Town of Whitehall ca. 1960 and now houses the Skenesborough Museum.
Now part of Peebles Island State Park.
Matton Shipyard (point of interest, NR Listed 2010 - not counted)
This nomination uses the New York State Canal Corporation's official tables of distances.
Operated by U.S. Army Corps of Engineers.
Initial construction began by Shanley-Morrissey, Inc. under Contract 71.
Contract 71-A (1/13/1913) to P. McGovern & Company.
Constructed by I.A. Hodge & Co under Contract 69.
Constructed by the Hudson River Power Transmission Company in 1897.
Bronk & Kimmey built concrete guide cribs at C3, C5, and C6 under Contract 168.
Retaining walls at the west (Saratoga County) end of the dam were built by E.M. Graves under Contract 73.
J.W. Holler of Fort Edward rehabilitated portions of Thompson Dam under maintenance contract M-23.
Collins Brothers erected a 16' x 30' frame freighthouse at Mechanicville Terminal in 1917.
D'Olier Engineering Company installed the hydroelectric generators, controls, and operating motors in 1911 under Contract 90.
Constructed 1906 by Empire Engineering Corporation under Barge Canal construction contract 1.
Constructed by Kingsbury Construction Company of Hudson Falls under Contract 24.
Scott Brothers of Rome NY restarted construction at the present location under Contract 54.
Hunkin-Conkey Construction Company of Cleveland took over Contract 54 in 1911.
Construction of Lock C7, C8 and the prism in-between was started by Kinser Construction Company under Contract 27.
Lock C11, the dam, and powerhouse building were built by Atlantic, Gulf and Pacific Company under Contract 15.
New York State Dredging Corporation of Rochester excavated the harbor and turning basin under Contract 7A.
The terminal warehouse was built by J.A. Laporte under Contract T201.
